title: St Mary and St Cuthbert, Chester-le-Street

infobox.value: is a [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") church in [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street"), [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). The site has been used for worship for over 1100 years; elements of the current building are over 950 years old. The oldest surviving translation of the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") into [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") was done here, by [Aldred](/wiki/Aldred_the_Scribe "Aldred the Scribe") between 947 and 968, at a time when it served as the centre of Christianity from [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian") to [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside").

The [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") of **St Mary and St Cuthbert** is a [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") church in [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street"), [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). The site has been used for worship for over 1100 years; elements of the current building are over 950 years old. The oldest surviving translation of the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") into [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") was done here, by [Aldred](/wiki/Aldred_the_Scribe "Aldred the Scribe") between 947 and 968, at a time when it served as the centre of Christianity from [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian") to [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside").

## St Cuthbert's community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: St Cuthbert's community")]

The church was established to house the body of [Cuthbert of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Cuthbert_of_Lindisfarne "Cuthbert of Lindisfarne"), [Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Bishop of Lindisfarne") from 684 to 687. After his death he became one of the most venerated saints of the time, with a significant [cultus](/wiki/Cult_(religious_practice) "Cult (religious practice)") and the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Venerable_Bede "Venerable Bede") writing both a verse and prose biography of him. So when driven out of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") by [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") raids in 875 the monks took [St Cuthbert's coffin](/wiki/St_Cuthbert%27s_coffin "St Cuthbert's coffin") along with other valuable items. They wandered for seven years before eventually settling at [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") (then called Cunecaster or Conceastre), at the site of the old Roman fort of [Concangis](/wiki/Concangis "Concangis"), in 883,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000333,_336–337-1) on land granted to them by [Guthred](/wiki/Guthred "Guthred").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow_Low1881107-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimeon,_Stevenson1855495,_664-3)

They built a wooden church and shrine for St Cuthbert's relics, dedicating it to St Mary and St Cuthbert. Though there was no shortage of stone in the ruins of Concangis they did not build a stone church; it has been suggested they did not intend to stay for as long as they eventually did. It was built within the Roman fort, which although abandoned over five hundred years before may have still offered some protection,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonner,_Standcliffe,_Rollason1989367–374-4) as well as access north and south along [Cade's Road](/wiki/Cade%27s_Road "Cade's Road") and to the sea by the [River Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear").

This was also a [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") as it contained the seat of the bishop, for the [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") (sometimes known as Lindisfarne and sometimes as [Cuncacestre](/wiki/Cuncacestre "Cuncacestre") the Latin name for Chester-le-Street) stretching between the boundaries of [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") at Teesside in the south, of [Alba](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Alba "Kingdom of Alba") at Lothian in the north and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") in the west. The bishop's authority was confirmed by [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great"),[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000338-5) and for the next 112 years the community was based here, visited by kings [Æthelstan](/wiki/%C3%86thelstan "Æthelstan") and [Edmund](/wiki/Edmund_I_of_England "Edmund I of England"), who both left gifts for the community, to add to the treasures brought from Lindisfarne.

Most notable among their treasures were the [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels"), created in Lindisfarne around 715. While here they were translated from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") into English, sometime between 947 and 968, by [Aldred](/wiki/Aldred_the_Scribe "Aldred the Scribe") writing a [gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(annotation) "Gloss (annotation)") in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") above the text, making them the oldest surviving English translation of the Gospels.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-BBC-8) The Gospels and St Cuthbert's coffin were here until 995, when renewed Viking raids drove the monks out, to [Ripon](/wiki/Ripon "Ripon") before returning to the more easily defended [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow_Low1881109-9) where they eventually built a [stone cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") around St Cuthbert's remains.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000338–340-10) The wooden church remained in place until replaced by a stone church in the mid 11th century.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonner,_Standcliffe,_Rollason1989367–374-4)

## Church building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Church building")]

[![Orange to light brown irregular weathered stones forming a corner of a building, two of the stones with filled in rectangular central holes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/LewisHoles_StMaryandStCuthbert.jpg/250px-LewisHoles_StMaryandStCuthbert.jpg)](/wiki/File:LewisHoles_StMaryandStCuthbert.jpg)

Stones with [lewis holes](/wiki/Lewis_(lifting_appliance) "Lewis (lifting appliance)") in a front buttress at bottom left and top right (now filled with mortar).

The oldest parts of the building that can be dated, to 1056 when a stone church was built to replace the wooden shrine to St Cuthbert, are the walls of the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") and the two largest pillars now near the centre of the [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave"). The church then would have been a third shorter and much narrower, as wide as the chancel today. [Lewis holes](/wiki/Lewis_(lifting_appliance) "Lewis (lifting appliance)") visible in two stones on a front [buttress](/wiki/Buttress "Buttress") show that Roman stone was used in later construction.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk200051-11)

The church was extended around 1267 with the [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave"), the lower part of the tower and east wall with [sedilia](/wiki/Sedilia "Sedilia") all dating from this time. In 1286 it was made a [collegiate church](/wiki/Collegiate_church "Collegiate church"),[[12]](#cite_note-KttP-12)[[13]](#cite_note-SMaSC-13) with a dean, seven canons, five chaplains and three deacons, supported by [tithes](/wiki/Tithes "Tithes") from extensive endowments throughout a large parish. Around 1383 an anchorage was added in one corner of the church, to be used by six (male) [anchorites](/wiki/Anchorites "Anchorites") until 1547, when it was extended. It is now the Ankers House Museum.[[14]](#cite_note-14) A 158 feet (48 m) [spire](/wiki/Spire "Spire") and [belfry](/wiki/Bell_tower "Bell tower") and three [bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") were added in 1409, one of which is still in use.[[12]](#cite_note-KttP-12)[[13]](#cite_note-SMaSC-13)

The collegiate church was dissolved during the [Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") and the church became a [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") with reduced wealth and influence. In subsequent years much of the money for building came from donations, with those of the [Lumley](/wiki/Earl_of_Scarbrough "Earl of Scarbrough") and [Lambton](/wiki/Earl_of_Durham "Earl of Durham") families particularly notable. [John Lumley](/wiki/John_Lumley,_1st_Baron_Lumley "John Lumley, 1st Baron Lumley") contributed a set of family [effigies](/wiki/Effigies "Effigies") that now lie along the north wall in 1595. A new south porch was added in 1742, while in 1829 a Lambton family pew by [Ignatius Bonomi](/wiki/Ignatius_Bonomi "Ignatius Bonomi") was added above a new vault (which once held the remains of [John Lambton](/wiki/John_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham "John Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham")) with an external staircase.[[12]](#cite_note-KttP-12)[[13]](#cite_note-SMaSC-13)

In 1862 major [restorations](/wiki/Victorian_restoration "Victorian restoration") were undertaken, and the church became a [rectory](/wiki/Rectory "Rectory") with the installation of an organ in 1865, later restored by [Harrison & Harrison](/wiki/Harrison_%26_Harrison "Harrison & Harrison").[[15]](#cite_note-15) To celebrate the church's millennium a screen was installed in 1883, along with other alterations. In 1927 a [reredos](/wiki/Reredos "Reredos"), panelling and a bishop's throne by [Sir Charles Nicholson](/wiki/Sir_Charles_Nicholson,_2nd_Baronet "Sir Charles Nicholson, 2nd Baronet") and three panels, *Journey of St Cuthbert's body*, by his brother [Archibald Keightley Nicholson](/wiki/Archibald_Keightley_Nicholson "Archibald Keightley Nicholson") were added. The church became a Grade I [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") in 1950, and doors were added to the South Porch in 1964.[[12]](#cite_note-KttP-12)[[13]](#cite_note-SMaSC-13)

Details of the exterior

* [![A wall of irregular stones, mostly light reddish brown, in which a large arch is set. This has been walled in, with a window in the centre, it also walled in, all in stone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/StMaryandStCuthbertAnchorageWindow.jpg/250px-StMaryandStCuthbertAnchorageWindow.jpg)](/wiki/File:StMaryandStCuthbertAnchorageWindow.jpg "Anchorage window")

  Anchorage window
* [![A dark painted wooden door, made of twelve vertical planks and dark iron hinges and nails, in an arched frame and wall of various light red and brown stones](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/StMaryandStCuthbertWalledUpArch.jpg/250px-StMaryandStCuthbertWalledUpArch.jpg)](/wiki/File:StMaryandStCuthbertWalledUpArch.jpg "North wall")

  North wall
* [![A small window with four leaded panes in a rectangle, another thin pane to the left of thee, in a worn stone-like frame, surrounded by sand coloured stones](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/StMaryandStCuthbertNorthDoor.jpg/250px-StMaryandStCuthbertNorthDoor.jpg)](/wiki/File:StMaryandStCuthbertNorthDoor.jpg "North door")

  North door

## Burials

## In art and literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In art and literature")]

The Lumley effigies are reflected upon in [Letitia Elizabeth Landon](/wiki/Letitia_Elizabeth_Landon "Letitia Elizabeth Landon")'s poetical illustration *The Aisle of Tombs* to an engraving of a painting by [Thomas Allom](/wiki/Thomas_Allom "Thomas Allom") in Fisher's Drawing Room Scrap Book, 1836: 'All their meaner part hath perished, In the earth at rest; And the present hour hath cherished What of them was best.'[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[The Aisle of Tombs,  
 a poetical illustration  
by L. E. L.](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Letitia_Elizabeth_Landon_(L._E._L.)_in_Fisher%27s_Drawing_Room_Scrap_Book,_1836/The_Aisle_of_Tombs "s:en:Letitia Elizabeth Landon (L. E. L.) in Fisher's Drawing Room Scrap Book, 1836/The Aisle of Tombs")**

## Millenary festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Millenary festival")]

> A thousand years! What a crowd of associations are suggested by these words. What thronging memories of the past, what solemn reflections of the present, what anxious hopes and fears for the future. A thousand years! What changes have taken place in this long lapse of time. How many nations have risen and fallen; how many tongues have died out; how many famous names have been forgotten. A thousand years ago!

[J. B. Lightfoot](/wiki/Joseph_Barber_Lightfoot "Joseph Barber Lightfoot"), [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), Sermon at the church Millenary Festival, 18 July 1883[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlunt1883198-17)

In 1883 the thousandth anniversary of the founding of the church was celebrated, from 18 July to 5 August. To mark the occasion the church was given a major facelift, with some new oak stalls and an oak screen for the chancel, a new [dossal](/wiki/Dossal "Dossal") and other hangings from designs by [C. E. Kempe](/wiki/Charles_Eamer_Kempe "Charles Eamer Kempe"), a new pulpit given by the [Earl of Scarbrough](/wiki/Earl_of_Scarbrough "Earl of Scarbrough"), a new marble pavement and other improvements, many of them provided as gifts for the occasion. Many other events took place over the festival, and a book was published to mark the church's first thousand years. The [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), [J. B. Lightfoot](/wiki/Joseph_Barber_Lightfoot "Joseph Barber Lightfoot") attended and took part in the celebrations, including holding a service for the dedication of the new bells.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlunt1883188–190-18)

Images from 1883[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlunt1883-19)

* [![The interior of a building, symmetric with two lines of tall pillars on either side, with candelabras and wooden benches between them.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/1883_StMaryandStCuthbertInterior.jpg/250px-1883_StMaryandStCuthbertInterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:1883_StMaryandStCuthbertInterior.jpg "The interior before renovations done in 1883.")

## Bells

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bells")]

The belfry at the church dates back over 600 years and originally housed three bells. A new [ring](/wiki/Ring_of_bells "Ring of bells") of six was cast by [John Taylor & Co](/wiki/John_Taylor_%26_Co "John Taylor & Co") of [Loughborough](/wiki/Loughborough "Loughborough") in 1883; two of the original bells were melted down and the metal included in the new ring. In 1908 two further bells (again by Taylor's) were added to the front of the ring to give eight, hung for [change ringing](/wiki/Change_ringing "Change ringing"). One of the original bells from 1409 still survives as an "odd ninth" and is used as a service bell: it is recognised as having national historic importance. The bells are used before services on Sundays, with practice on Friday evenings.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlunt1883189-22)

## Lindisfarne Gospels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Lindisfarne Gospels")]

[![A single page of illuminated manuscript in Latin.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/LindisfarneFol27rIncipitMatt.jpg/250px-LindisfarneFol27rIncipitMatt.jpg)](/wiki/File:LindisfarneFol27rIncipitMatt.jpg)

Opening page the [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") from the Lindisfarne Gospels.

The [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels") were kept at Durham until 1539, when during the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") St Cuthbert's shrine there was looted and they were taken to [London](/wiki/London "London"). Today they are kept at the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library"), but a [facsimile](/wiki/Facsimile "Facsimile") copy is kept at the church and can be viewed when the church is open.[[8]](#cite_note-BBC-8)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Anchorage and Ankers House Museum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Anchorage and Ankers House Museum")]

[![Stone building with two sloping wings, the left wider than the right; large stained glass window above door in the center, topped by lower part of tower](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/StMaryandStCuthbertFront.jpg/250px-StMaryandStCuthbertFront.jpg)](/wiki/File:StMaryandStCuthbertFront.jpg)

Front view of the church, with Ankers House Museum on the left.

Attached to the church is the former anchorage, one of the few surviving to this day and described as the most complete example of its kind in England. It was created by blocking off one corner of a church in the late 14th century, with an extra room added externally in the 16th century. Originally it was on two levels, but the floor was removed at some point to allow more space and light. From 1383 to 1547 it was occupied by six [anchorites](/wiki/Anchorites "Anchorites"), each being walled in to the anchorage for life, able to watch services through a [squint](/wiki/Hagioscope "Hagioscope") into the church which looks down onto a side altar, being fed through another slit to the outside.

It was used in this way until the [reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"). The anchorage then became a place sporadically occupied by the poor or members of the church.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlunt188313–14-25) In 1986 it became the **Ankers House Museum**, one of the smallest museums in the UK. It shows the conditions that an anchorite lived in when it was occupied, as well as containing [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") and [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") items found on the site.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
